summary: Before I was drafted, I took the Army Air Corps written exam and surprisingly passed it. However, during my physical, I failed due to poor depth perception, which disqualified me from joining. It was a disappointment because I initially wanted to be part of the Air Corps, but I ended up in the Army instead.

I grew up in Chicago and attended high school there before being drafted. My induction into the service happened in Chicago at the office on Van Buren Street. It was my home and the place where my military journey began.

The Army draft office on Van Buren Street in Chicago was where I received my notification to serve. I took the streetcar there when I was about eighteen or nineteen years old. That was the starting point of my military service.
After being drafted, I was sent to Camp Grant in Illinois. From there, I took a troop train to Camp Walters in Texas for basic training. Camp Grant was my first military station after induction.

Illinois was home to Camp Grant where I started my military journey after being drafted in Chicago. It was the first stop before heading down to Texas for further training. Illinois marked the beginning of my Army experience.

Camp Walters in Texas was where I completed my basic infantry training. It was a tough three-month period, especially since I wasn't athletic and was considered a klutz. Despite that, I endured and learned the basics of Army life there.

Texas was where much of my training took place after basic at Camp Walters. I was sent to several locations including Camp Howze near Gainesville and even briefly to parachute school at Fort Benning, Georgia, before returning to Texas. The state was central to my preparation for combat.

I attended Marshall High School in Chicago, where I was more interested in music than sports. I wasn't athletic and even failed the swimming test there. Marshall was part of my early life before joining the service.

I also attended Manley High School in Chicago, alongside Marshall. Between the two, I was known more for my music interest than athletics. My experiences there shaped my youth before the Army drafted me.

After failing the Air Corps physical, I chose to join the Army since I couldn't swim and didn't want the Navy or Marines. I was conscripted in 1943 and trained extensively in infantry tactics, heavy weapons, and communications. The Army was where I spent the entirety of my World War II service.

I considered the Navy as an option when I had to choose between Army, Navy, or Marines after failing the Air Corps physical. However, I didn't know how to swim and was pulled out of the pool during high school swimming tests, so I ruled out the Navy. It was never really an option for me.

The Marines were also an option when I had to pick a branch after failing the Air Corps physical. But I chose the Army instead, partly because of my inability to swim and my lack of enthusiasm for the Marines. So I never served in the Marine Corps.

Due to a clerical error, I was sent to Camp Benning, Georgia, for parachute school even though I wasn't supposed to be there. The training was extremely physical and grueling, and I was not happy about it. Eventually, I was redirected back to Texas.

I attended parachute school at Camp Benning, which was a brutal and physically demanding experience. I was there due to a mistake and was not originally intended to be in that program. Despite my fears and physical limitations, I completed most of the training before being reassigned.

Camp Howze in Texas was my home division base after parachute school. It was near Gainesville and close to towns like Mineral Wells and Weatherford. The 103rd Infantry Division had recently moved there from Camp Claiborne, Louisiana, and I trained there with them.

I was assigned to the 103rd Infantry Division after parachute school, training at Camp Howze in Texas. My brother had just finished his training with the same division at Camp Claiborne, Louisiana, before I joined. We both survived the war, although we never saw each other until after it ended.

Camp Claiborne, Louisiana, was where my brother trained with the 103rd Infantry Division before it moved to Camp Howze. I joined the division three weeks after he finished there. It was part of our family's connection to the Army during the war.

Gainesville, Texas, was the city near Camp Howze where I was stationed with the 103rd Infantry Division. It was a small town surrounded by other places like Mineral Wells and Weatherford. This area was central to my training and preparation for combat.

Mineral Wells, Texas, was a nearby town to Camp Howze where I trained. It was known for its 'crazy water crystals,' which we jokingly called 'Venereal Wells.' It was part of the local geography during my time in Texas.

Weatherford, Texas, was another small town near Camp Howze where I was stationed. It was notable as the hometown of Mary Martin, a famous person from the area. The town still had wooden sidewalks when I was there.
Mary Martin was from Weatherford, Texas, near where I trained at Camp Howze. I remember the town had wooden sidewalks back then. She was a notable figure from that area during my time there.

Dallas, Texas, was one of the larger cities near where I trained at Camp Howze. We called it 'Big D,' and it was a regional hub compared to smaller towns like Denton. It was part of the broader Texas environment during my service.

Fort Worth, Texas, was another major city close to Camp Howze where I was stationed. Along with Dallas and Denton, it formed part of the regional landscape during my military training. It was familiar territory during my time in Texas.

Denton, Texas, was known as 'Little D' and was home to a women's teachers' school near where I trained. It was close to Camp Howze and part of the local area I got to know. The girls there were beautiful, though they didn't pay much attention to me.

I was drafted into the Army during World War II in 1943 and underwent extensive training in Texas and Georgia. I served in the 103rd Infantry Division and survived the war along with my brother. The war shaped my entire military experience and life.

During my time in the Army, I experienced a lot of anti-Semitism, especially early on. It was a demographic thing and made my training harder since I wasn't very athletic. I fought back when provoked, even once injuring a soldier who was harassing me at Camp Walters.
The transcript does not mention the PX, so I have no personal story or experience related to it during my service.
The transcript does not provide specific information about the 409th Infantry Regiment in relation to my service.
I have no direct experience or mention of the 410th Infantry Regiment in my military story.
The 411th Infantry Regiment was not specifically mentioned in my account, so I cannot provide a summary about it.

In my heavy weapons platoon, I was often the wire man, but the Browning Automatic Rifle (BAR) was the key firepower in our squad. The BAR man was part of the nucleus of the rifle squad along with scouts and the point man. We all trained to be interchangeable with weapons like the BAR to maintain unit efficiency.
The 42nd Infantry Division was not mentioned in my military experience or training, so I have no personal connection to it.
